sources.title: Dutch Authorities Dismantle Botnet Linked to 17 Million Infected Devices
WP Maps Pro bug exploited to create admin accounts on WordPress sites
Security Affairs newsletter Round 579 by Pierluigi Paganini – INTERNATIONAL EDITION
27,000-Download Codex UI Tool Secretly Stole OpenAI Refresh Tokens
CVE-2026-0257: Rapid7 Caught Attackers Abusing Forged VPN Cookies Against Multiple Customers

executive_brief:
This week, the threat landscape pivoted from opportunistic exploitation to systematic supply chain and perimeter subversion. Attackers are poisoning trusted update channels, compromising developer tooling, and forging authentication tokens to bypass enterprise defenses. The simultaneous active exploitation of FortiClient EMS update mechanisms, a widespread developer UI tool, and Palo Alto GlobalProtect VPN appliances reveals a coordinated push to undermine the security infrastructure organizations rely on for endpoint management and remote access. Security teams can no longer assume vendor patches or developer dependencies are safe; they are now primary attack vectors.

Compounding this infrastructure assault, mass compromises of Ghost CMS and WordPress plugins are being weaponized to distribute ClickFix malware and establish persistent admin backdoors. Attackers are chaining unauthenticated web flaws to hijack user sessions and pivot into broader network environments. While Dutch authorities successfully dismantled a botnet controlling 17 million devices, the underlying tactics exposing networks remain largely unpatched. The window for reactive patching has closed. Security operations must immediately treat update channels, developer dependencies, and VPN authentication as hostile by default.

threat_stories.headline: The Poisoned Pipeline: Attackers Weaponize Developer Tools and Security Updates
threat_stories.narrative:
Threat actors are executing a deliberate assault on the software development and security update supply chains. A widely distributed Codex UI tool, downloaded over 27,000 times, secretly exfiltrated OpenAI refresh tokens from developer machines. Simultaneously, operators are exploiting a critical flaw in FortiClient EMS to distribute the EKZ Infostealer disguised as legitimate vendor patches. This is not random opportunism; it is a calculated strike against the trust chains that modern development and endpoint management depend on. By compromising trusted channels, attackers bypass traditional perimeter defenses and land directly inside development environments and enterprise endpoint fleets.

The exploitation lifecycle moves with precision. Attackers identify high-value developer tools and security management consoles, inject malicious payloads, and leverage existing update mechanisms for distribution. The FortiClient EMS campaign exploits user trust in automated patching, while the Codex UI compromise targets AI integration workflows. Both campaigns deliver credential harvesters that establish persistence and enable lateral movement. Security teams face a dual threat: compromised development credentials and poisoned endpoint management infrastructure.

This campaign forces a fundamental shift in security posture. Organizations must verify every update against vendor-signed checksums before deployment. Developer tooling requires strict network segmentation and runtime monitoring. The assumption that vendor channels are safe is dead. Attackers have proven they will weaponize the very tools meant to secure and accelerate operations.

timeline.event: Codex UI tool begins distributing with hidden OpenAI token-stealing code
FortiClient EMS exploit kits deployed, masquerading as vendor patches
timeline.significance: Triggers widespread EKZ Infostealer distribution across enterprise endpoints

timeline.event: Security researchers confirm active exploitation across multiple environments
timeline.significance: Validates supply chain poisoning as a coordinated campaign

threat_stories.impact_assessment: Software development teams face immediate credential theft and unauthorized AI model access. Organizations using FortiClient EMS risk enterprise-wide infostealer deployment, credential harvesting, and endpoint management bypass.
threat_stories.action_required: Disable automatic FortiClient EMS updates immediately. Manually verify all patch signatures against Fortinet's official repository. Audit third-party developer tools for unexpected outbound connections. Isolate compromised development workstations and rotate all API credentials.

threat_stories.headline: The Perimeter Breach: VPN Cookie Forgery and Botnet Fallout
threat_stories.narrative:
Enterprise VPN defenses are collapsing under a cryptographic misconfiguration that attackers exploit with surgical precision. CVE-2026-0257 in Palo Alto GlobalProtect allows threat actors to forge authentication cookies when the same certificate handles both HTTPS services and cookie encryption. Attackers extract the public key from standard web sessions, craft valid admin cookies, and walk straight into internal networks without credentials. This vulnerability shatters the foundational trust assumption in remote access infrastructure. The exploitation requires no user interaction and bypasses multi-factor authentication entirely.

The perimeter crisis intersects with broader infrastructure compromise. Dutch authorities executed a major operation dismantling a botnet spanning 17 million infected devices. While this law enforcement action removes a significant DDoS and scanning capability, it simultaneously proves that edge infrastructure remains heavily compromised. Attackers pivot quickly from seized botnet nodes to direct exploitation of vulnerable VPN gateways. The combination of forged authentication tokens and compromised edge infrastructure creates a perfect storm for unauthorized network access.

Organizations relying on Palo Alto GlobalProtect face immediate exposure. The misconfiguration is widespread, and the exploitation technique is trivial to automate. Security teams must treat all VPN authentication logs as compromised until cryptographic isolation is enforced. The perimeter is no longer a boundary; it is a launchpad for attackers who understand how to weaponize trust.

timeline.event: Rapid7 researchers identify cookie forgery technique in GlobalProtect deployments
timeline.significance: Exposes widespread cryptographic misconfiguration in enterprise VPNs

timeline.event: Proof-of-concept script published, triggering immediate wild exploitation
timeline.significance: Accelerates unauthorized VPN access campaigns across multiple sectors

timeline.event: Dutch authorities seize infrastructure controlling 17 million IoT/edge devices
timeline.significance: Demonstrates scale of compromised edge infrastructure fueling perimeter attacks

threat_stories.impact_assessment: Any organization using Palo Alto GlobalProtect with the shared certificate misconfiguration faces immediate unauthorized network access. The botnet takedown reduces DDoS capacity but highlights persistent edge compromise enabling VPN bypass campaigns.
threat_stories.action_required: Immediately audit PAN-OS certificate configurations. Isolate the HTTPS and cookie encryption certificates using dedicated keys. Deploy the patched PAN-OS version without delay. Implement strict network segmentation for VPN gateways and monitor for anomalous admin session creation.

threat_stories.headline: The CMS Takedown: Mass Compromise and Malware Distribution
threat_stories.narrative:
Web content management systems have become the preferred launchpad for automated malware distribution. A critical flaw in Ghost CMS (CVE-2026-26980) is being actively exploited to inject ClickFix malware into hundreds of compromised sites. Simultaneously, an unauthenticated vulnerability in the WP Maps Pro WordPress plugin allows attackers to spawn rogue admin accounts with zero user interaction. Threat actors are chaining these exploits to establish persistent backdoors, hijack user sessions, and pivot into broader network environments. The shift from targeted web application attacks to automated CMS exploitation reflects a move toward volume-based infrastructure abuse.

The attack pattern is ruthlessly efficient. Automated scanners identify vulnerable Ghost and WordPress instances, deploy exploit scripts, and establish administrative control within minutes. Attackers then inject ClickFix payloads that hijack visitor sessions, harvest credentials, and distribute additional malware. The WP Maps Pro vulnerability bypasses authentication entirely, granting immediate administrative access. This combination turns public-facing websites into weaponized distribution nodes.

Organizations running these platforms face complete site takeover and downstream compromise of their visitors. The exploitation is active, widespread, and requires zero interaction. Security teams must treat all public-facing CMS deployments as compromised until verified. Web application firewalls alone are insufficient; organizations must enforce strict access controls, validate plugin integrity, and monitor for unauthorized administrative activity.

timeline.event: Ghost CMS vulnerability disclosed, exploit kits deployed within hours
timeline.significance: Triggers automated mass-compromise campaigns targeting Ghost instances

timeline.event: WP Maps Pro admin creation bug begins active scanning campaigns
timeline.significance: Enables unauthenticated WordPress site takeover at scale

timeline.event: ClickFix malware distribution peaks across compromised Ghost instances
timeline.significance: Transforms compromised CMS platforms into active malware distribution nodes

timeline.event: Security researchers confirm widespread WordPress admin takeover
timeline.significance: Validates CMS exploitation as a primary vector for credential theft and persistence

threat_stories.impact_assessment: Organizations running Ghost CMS or WP Maps Pro face complete site takeover, visitor credential theft, and active malware distribution. Compromised sites serve as pivot points for broader network intrusion.
threat_stories.action_required: Disable WP Maps Pro immediately across all WordPress deployments. Update Ghost CMS to the latest patched version. Implement WAF rules blocking ClickFix payloads and unauthorized user creation endpoints. Rotate all CMS credentials and audit server files for unauthorized modifications.

actor_profiles.name: EKZ Supply Chain Campaign Operators
actor_profiles.motivation: Financial theft, credential harvesting, persistent access
actor_profiles.recent_activity: Weaponizing FortiClient EMS update mechanisms to distribute EKZ Infostealer disguised as legitimate vendor patches. Simultaneously compromising developer tooling to steal AI refresh tokens and API credentials.

actor_profiles.name: ClickFix Web Exploitation Group
actor_profiles.motivation: Financial gain, ad fraud, credential harvesting, malware distribution
actor_profiles.recent_activity: Automated exploitation of Ghost CMS and WP Maps Pro to establish administrative backdoors and inject ClickFix malware. Leveraging unauthenticated flaws for rapid site takeover and visitor session hijacking.

critical_vulnerabilities.description: Critical vulnerability in FortiClient EMS allows threat actors to execute malicious code. Attackers actively exploit this flaw to distribute the EKZ Infostealer by disguising malicious payloads as legitimate Fortinet software updates, bypassing security controls and achieving persistence.
critical_vulnerabilities.remediation: Apply the latest security patch provided by Fortinet. Verify digital signatures of all EMS updates before installation. Disable automatic updates until integrity verification is enforced.

critical_vulnerabilities.description: Vulnerability in Ghost CMS enables mass compromise of websites. Threat actors leverage this flaw to deploy ClickFix malware campaigns, hijacking user sessions and injecting malicious scripts into compromised CMS instances without authentication.
critical_vulnerabilities.remediation: Update Ghost CMS to the latest patched version immediately. Deploy WAF rules to block known ClickFix payloads. Restrict admin panel access via IP whitelisting and enable rate limiting.

critical_vulnerabilities.description: Authentication bypass vulnerability in Palo Alto GlobalProtect allows attackers to forge VPN auth cookies. When a common misconfiguration exists (shared certificate for HTTPS and cookie encryption), attackers extract the public key and craft valid cookies for any user, including local admin, bypassing authentication entirely.
critical_vulnerabilities.remediation: Upgrade to a patched version of PAN-OS immediately. Generate and use a dedicated certificate exclusively for cookie encryption. Disable the authentication override feature until patched.

critical_vulnerabilities.description: Unauthenticated admin account creation vulnerability in WP Maps Pro allows attackers to spawn rogue administrator accounts. The flaw bypasses authentication mechanisms, granting full administrative control over the compromised WordPress site without prior credentials.
critical_vulnerabilities.remediation: Disable the WP Maps Pro plugin immediately. Implement WAF rules to block unauthorized user creation endpoints. Monitor WordPress user logs for unexpected account creation events and rotate all administrative credentials.

hunting_leads.title: Detect FortiClient EMS Malicious Patch Execution
hunting_leads.context: Attackers disguise EKZ Infostealer as legitimate Fortinet updates. Hunting for unexpected process execution during patch deployment windows identifies compromised endpoints before lateral movement occurs.
hunting_leads.query: | where process_name contains "FortiClient" OR process_name contains "EMS" | where event_id = 1 OR event_id = 4688 | where file_hash NOT IN (SELECT file_hash FROM known_good_fortinet_patches) | stats count by process_name, file_hash, parent_process, user
indicators: Unexpected outbound connections to non-Fortinet IPs during update windows
Process execution from temp directories during EMS updates

hunting_leads.title: Identify Ghost CMS and WP Maps Pro Admin Takeover
hunting_leads.context: Automated exploitation creates rogue admin accounts and injects ClickFix payloads. Monitoring for unauthorized user creation and script injection reveals compromised CMS instances.
hunting_leads.query: | where log_source = "wordpress" OR log_source = "ghost_cms" | where event_type = "user_created" OR event_type = "admin_login" | where user_role = "administrator" AND auth_method != "sso" | stats count by username, source_ip, timestamp
indicators: ClickFix JavaScript payloads in web server logs
Sudden spike in user creation API calls

hunting_leads.title: Detect Palo Alto VPN Cookie Forgery and Unauthorized Access
hunting_leads.context: CVE-2026-0257 exploitation bypasses authentication via forged cookies. Hunting for anomalous VPN sessions and admin logins without corresponding MFA events identifies compromised gateways.
hunting_leads.query: | where log_source = "panos_globalprotect" | where event_type = "vpn_connection_established" | where auth_method = "cookie" AND mfa_status != "success" | stats count by username, source_ip, gateway_ip, timestamp
indicators: VPN connections authenticated via cookie without MFA
Admin sessions originating from unusual geolocations
Rapid succession of VPN connection attempts from single IPs

hunting_leads.title: Track Codex UI Tool Token Exfiltration
hunting_leads.context: Compromised developer tool silently harvests OpenAI refresh tokens. Monitoring for unexpected API token usage and outbound connections to unknown endpoints catches supply chain compromise.
hunting_leads.query: | where process_name contains "codex" OR process_name contains "ui_tool" | where network_destination NOT IN (known_developer_endpoints) | where request_headers contains "Authorization: Bearer" OR request_headers contains "refresh_token" | stats count by process_name, network_destination, timestamp
indicators: OpenAI refresh tokens transmitted to non-OpenAI domains
Unexpected outbound HTTPS connections from developer workstations
API token usage spikes from compromised machine IPs

statistics.key_changes: Active exploitation has shifted from opportunistic web scanning to systematic supply chain poisoning and perimeter authentication bypass. Security update mechanisms and developer tooling are now primary attack vectors.
emerging_trends: CMS mass compromise campaigns for automated malware distribution
VPN cryptographic misconfiguration exploitation bypassing MFA
Developer tool supply chain poisoning targeting AI credentials
